one.en: PROPERTY – Where both parties seek orders adjusting their property interests – Dispute about composition of the asset pool – Where the husband contend…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where both parties seek orders adjusting their property interests – Dispute about composition of the asset pool – Where the husband contends asserted loans from his parents should be included in the pool of assets for division between the parties – Held the purported loan agreement between the husband and his parents was of recent invention – Where the parties were in dispute about the extent of their respective contributions and future needs – Held contributions and future needs roughly equal – Treatment of prior distributions made to the parties under s 78(5) and a monetary adjustment made in favour of the wife – Where both parties sought to retain the former family home the wife had lived since separation – Held wife to retain former family home subject to her capacity to refinance the mortgage encumbering the property – Default order for the husband to receive the property and pay a sum to the wife if she is unable to retain the property – Superannuation equalised in accordance with agreed position of the parties

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

Williams J

INTRODUCTION

By an Initiating Application filed 14 June 2022, which was amended for the sixth time on 22 September 2025, the applicant husband seeks a property adjustment pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). The respondent wife filed a Response to Final orders on 29 July 2022 and a Further Amended Response to Initiating Application on 4 February 2025.
The parties’ relationship and marriage spanned just over 11 years. There is one child of the marriage, a son aged 15. In February 2025, the parties entered into final parenting orders, which provide for the child to live with the husband and spend time with the wife in accordance with his wishes.
The parties have a modest pool of assets for division between them comprising three properties and some superannuation entitlements.
For the reasons that follow, I have determined the asset pool as at the date of the trial should be divided in the proportion of 46.69 percent to the husband and 53.31 percent to the wife.

BACKGROUND

The husband is aged 41 and is employed as a professional. The wife is aged 40 and was formerly employed in multiple casual retail roles before securing a full-time role as a public servant and later moving to N Company. She left that employment in early 2025 and at the date of the trial was not employed, although she was attempting to obtain employment. Both parties were born in India.
In 2009, the parties married and commenced cohabitation at the home of the husband’s parents in India.
In 2010, their son was born, and they continued to live with the husband’s parents. In 2014, the husband applied for permanent residency in Australia as a skilled applicant. He entered Australia in early 2015, with the wife and child following in mid-2015. Both parties agree during the time they lived with the husband’s parents, they received regular assistance with their domestic responsibilities.
The husband asserts, and the wife denies, in May 2015 the husband’s father lent them $4,500.
In late 2015, the parties purchased a property at D Street, Suburb E (“the [D Street] property”). The husband is the sole registered proprietor of the property. The property was purchased for $220,000 together with $7,699 for mortgage insurance. The purchase was funded by a mortgage of $208,000, cash of $26,484 withdrawn from the husband’s bank accounts and according to the husband, but denied by the wife, the sum of $6,357 lent by the husband’s father.
In November 2015, the husband’s parents travelled to Australia and in December, the husband, wife, child and husband’s parents moved in the D Street property. In late 2016 the husbands’ parents returned to India.
In late 2017, the husband and wife entered into a contract to purchase vacant land at B Street, Suburb C (“the [B Street] property”). The B Street property was purchased in joint names for the sum of $162,000 which was financed by a mortgage from a National Australia Bank of $128,968 and joint savings of $39,753.54, which was withdrawn from the husband’s bank account. In December 2017 the parties entered into a contract to build a home on the B Street property.
The husband asserts he obtained a loan from his father on 28th of May 2018 for $49,975 to purchase a property situated at F Street, Suburb E (“the [F Street] property”). The wife denies the amount advanced was a loan.
The husband asserts that in June 2018 he obtained a further loan from his father of $15,000 for construction of the B Street property. He also asserts obtaining a further loan of $15,000 in cash from his father on 18 June 2018. The wife denies either amount was a loan from the husband’s father.
The husband alleges that he executed a loan agreement between himself and his parents in the presence of third-party witnesses on 27 September 2018. A copy of the loan agreement is Exhibit A-6. They wife challenges the veracity of the loan agreement and contends she did not know about the agreement until the commencement of these proceedings.
The construction of a four-bedroom home on the land at B Street property commenced in June 2019 and the family commenced living there in December 2019.
In February 2020, the husband's parents again visited Australia and resided at the B Street property with the family. The husband asserts his father assisted him to build a front and rear deck at the property and landscaped the property. The husband contends he obtained a loan from his father of $19,000, which was utilised for the purchase of materials for landscaping works and construction of the decks at the property. The wife denies the husband's father lent them the funds, as asserted by the husband.
In May 2020, the husband completed his Drink and Drug Driver Behaviour Change program.
In June/July 2020, the parties drew down on the equity in their three properties and the sum of $141,331.55 was deposited into a joint offset account.
In mid-2020, the parties entered into a contract to purchase a property situated at O Street Suburb P (“the [O Street] property”) for the sum of $327,500. The sum of $10,000, being a partial deposit for the property, was paid from the husband’s bank account.
In January 2021, the husband commenced operating “[M Company]”, from the B Street property.
In June 2021, the parties purchased a vehicle, which is registered in the respondent’s name, but the parties disagree as to whether the respondent was the primary user of the vehicle.
In August 2021, the parties obtain a second refinance, refinancing the D Street, B Street and F Street properties and received $309,645.10, which was subsequently deposited into a joint off-set account.
The wife gave her salary to the husband between May 2011 and April 2022.
In late April 2022, the parties separated after the wife filed an application for an intervention Order and in June 2022, the husband commenced proceedings in Division 2 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia. The matter was transferred to this Division by orders dated 20 December 2023.
Between December 2019 and January 2023, the husband utilised the rent from both the D Street and F Street properties for his own use.
Between 28 April 2022 and December 2023, the mortgage repayments for all the properties were paid by funds in the joint offset account obtained from both refinances. The wife also asserts the husband utilised the offset account to fund other personal expenses, including expenses referable to the rental properties. The husband denies that he did so.
After various interim hearings the trial commenced on 13 October 2025. Subsequent to the conclusion of the trial, the calculations for Capital Gains Tax (“CGT”) were forwarded to my chambers on 26 November 2025.

THE PROPERTY PROPOSALS OF THE PARTIES

The husband’s proposal

The orders which the husband sought from the Court are Annexure A to these reasons. He sought the non-superannuation asset pool be adjusted 60 percent in his favour and 40 percent in favour of the wife and that his superannuation entitlements be split in favour of the wife to effect an equal division of their joint superannuation.

Documents relied upon by the husband

The husband relied upon the following documents:
(a) Trial affidavit of the husband filed 22 September 2025

(b) Affidavit in Reply of the husband filed 7 October 2025

(c) Financial Statement of the husband filed 22 September 2025

(d) Further (sixth) Amended Application for Final Orders filed 22 September 2025

(e) Affidavit of the husband filed 31 October 2024

(f) Enforcement Application filed 31 October 2024

(g) Undertaking as to Disclosure filed 27 March 2024

(h) Affidavit of Mr Diamanto filed 22 March 2024

(i) Affidavit of Mr Q filed 27 November 2023

(j) Affidavit of Mr R filed 17 July 2023

(k) Affidavit of Mr S filed 4 July 2023

(l) Affidavit of the husband filed 22 March 2024

(m) Affidavit of Mr Q filed 27 November 2023

(n) Financial questionnaire filed 15 September 2022

(o) Affidavit of Mr T filed 22 August 2024

(p) Outline of Case document filed 9 October 2025

(q) Documents tendered by counsel, including documents produced pursuant to subpoena.

The wife’s proposal

The orders which the wife sought from the court are set out at Annexure B to these reasons. She sought the non-superannuation asset pool be adjusted 10% in her favour and the husband’s superannuation entitlements be split to effect an equal division of their joint superannuation.

Documents relied upon by the respondent wife

The wife relied upon the following documents:
(a) Further amended response to initiating application filed 4 February 2025

(b) Financial statement of Ms Reyyan filed 4 February 2025

(c) Affidavit of the wife filed 29 September 2025

(d) Financial statement filed 29 September 2025

(e) Undertaking as to disclosure filed 29 September 2025

(f) Outline of Case document filed 9 October 2025

(g) Documents tendered by counsel, including documents produced pursuant to subpoena.

The following documents were tendered during the trial by counsel for the applicant:
Exhibit number
	
DESCRIPTION

The following documents were tendered during the trial by counsel for the respondent:

Husband bank statements 27 April 2021 – 29 June 2021

EVIDENCE

The standard of proof in this case is the balance of probabilities (s 140 Evidence Act 1995 (Cth)).
Section 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) provides:
(1) In a civil proceeding, the court must find the case of a party proved if it is satisfied that the case has been proved on the balance of probabilities.

(2) Without limiting the matters that the court may take into account in deciding whether it is so satisfied, it is to take into account:

(a) the nature of the cause of action or defence; and

(b) the nature of the subject matter of the proceeding; and

(c) the gravity of the matters alleged.

Both parties relied upon their affidavits and Financial Statements. The trial affidavits exhaustively recounted the history of the parties’ relationship and the dispute. I have examined that evidence and do not propose to repeat it in these reasons.
A judge is not required to make an explicit finding on each disputed piece of evidence (Housing Commission of New South Wales v Tatmar Pastoral Co Pty Ltd [1983] 3 NSWLR 378 at 386).
Nor is it necessary for a trial judge to mention every fact or argument relied on as relevant to an issue in the proceeding. In Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon [2003] HCA 48; (2003) 200 ALR 447 at [62], Gleeson CJ, McHugh and Gummow JJ said:
...A judge’s reasons are not required to mention every fact or argument relied on by the losing party as relevant to an issue. Judgments of trial judges would soon become longer than they already are if a judge’s failure to mention such facts and arguments would be evidence that he or she had not properly considered the losing party’s case.

Credibility of witnesses

The husband was cross examined by counsel for the wife. He was not an impressive witness and was not always responsive to the question asked. Some of his answers defied logic and seemed to be made up as he went. An example was when he sought to blame a finance broker for his answer as to why he did not disclose the alleged loan from his parents on his application to the bank for finance. Similarly, his lack of credible explanation why the purported loan agreement with his parents was written on a piece of paper ostensibly extracted from a diary dated February 2008, when the agreement was dated 2018, leads me to treat his evidence with caution. Where his evidence differs from that of the wife, I generally prefer her evidence.
The husband’s father was cross examined by counsel for the wife with the assistance of an interpreter, although it was evident he understood some English. His evidence was even more problematic than the husband. He did not answer the question asked and used his answers to questions as an opportunity to launch into a speech about an unrelated topic which he thought would advance his son’s case. He did or could not provide credible explanations for many questions asked of him. I place little weight on his evidence.
The wife was cross examined by counsel for the husband. She impressed me as responsive, direct and making a concerted effort to tell the truth. She made appropriate concessions, such as for example when shown the husband’s bank accounts into which he asserted his father had deposited funds, she agreed that the deposits had been made. She was a consistent historian and was obviously emotionally adversely affected by questions pertaining to her future earning capacity, because of the impact on her mental health of the loss of her relationship with her son. I accept her as truthful and prefer her evidence to that of the husband and his father.

RELEVANT LEGISLATION

The trial of this property proceeding began after 10 June 2025, when the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) commenced. The relevant amendments apply to the determination of this application.
Under the new provisions of Part VIII of the Act, it is first necessary to identify the existing legal and equitable property interests and liabilities of the parties (s 79(3)(a)), which will then inform whether it is just and equitable to make any adjustment at all (s 79(2)).
If the court determines a property adjustment is warranted, then the court must take into account the parties’ respective contributions (s 79(3)(b)(i) and s 79(4) and their current and future circumstances (s 79(3)(b) (ii) and s79(5)).
Turning now to the parties existing property interests and liabilities.

THE PARTIES’ EXISTING INTERESTS IN PROPERTY AND LIABILITIES

The parties provided two joint balance sheets of their assets and liabilities as at the date of trial, which included notional property and some disputed items. The first balance sheet included minor items which had not been valued and other disputed items, which the parties agreed should be deleted. The second joint balance sheet, which is set out below, also included some disputed items.

Notional addbacks

Both the husband and the wife received interim property distributions from the sale of a property of $125,896.61. The first joint balance sheet sought to include these amounts as notional addbacks. Following the amendments to Part VIII of the Act, interim property distributions are no longer notionally added back to the parties existing assets. The practice was recently considered by the Full Court in Shinohara v Shinohara (2025) FLC 94-266 (“Shinohara”) at [95] – [101], [117] – [128] and [135(d)]. Prior interim distributions are now to be considered under s 79(5) of the Act. The second Amended Joint Balance Sheet excluded the notional addbacks.
In this case the husband had depleted the funds distributed to him, so that none remained as at the date of trial. The wife had also expended a part of the interim distribution and her funds remaining as at the date of the trial amounted to $63,000, which was included in the second Amended Joint Balance Sheet.
Accordingly, $63,000 will be included as an asset of the wife. The sum of $125,896 will be taken into account under s 79(5) of the Act as a prior distribution to the husband and $62,896, being the amount expended by the wife, will also be taken into account as a prior distribution to the wife.

Loans from husband’s parents – Items 14 and 15

The parties remained in dispute about whether sums allegedly advanced by the husband’s father and/or parents were loans. The husband contended the total amount owing to his father, inclusive of interest, as at the date of trial was $125,129.44. The husband contended he had partially repaid $38,800 of the asserted loan from his prior property distribution and therefore $86,329.44 remained outstanding to his father. The husband claimed the funds so advanced were the subject of a loan agreement entered into between himself and his father on 27 September 2018.
The husband also claimed a further sum of $19,000 in cash was advanced to the parties in 2020 and was applied to landscaping of the B Street property. According to him, each of his parents brought $10,000 cash into Australia. That advance was obviously not included in the loan agreement and there was no apparent claim this advance should attract interest, other than the husband’s evidence during cross examination, there was an expectation interest would be paid, but there was no actual discussion about interest.
The husband claimed his father advanced various sums to the parties as follows:
(a) $6,500 in February 2015;

(b) $4,500 cash deposited into the husband’s NAB account on 11 May 2015;[3]

(c) $6,357 transferred into the husband’s NAB account on 29 September 2015;[4]

(d) $49,750 transferred into the husband’s NAB account on 28 May 2018;[5]

(e) $6,000 cash deposited into the husband’s NAB account on 18 June 2018;[6]

(f) $7,500 cash deposited into the wife’s NAB account on 18 June 2018.[7]

The husband’s evidence was the amounts advanced by his father were applied as follows:
(a) $6,357 was applied to the purchase of the D Street property;

(b) $49,750 was applied to assist with the purchase of the F Street property;

(c) $15,000 cash which was divided and deposited into the bank accounts of the parties for the construction of the B Street property.[8]

In support of his contention the amounts advanced were a loan repayable to his father, the husband relied on a Loan Agreement dated 27 September 2018.[9]
The Loan Agreement is a handwritten document, which the husband said was written by him, but dictated to him by an accountant. Curiously, although the document is dated 27 September 2018, it is written on what appears to be two pages from a diary dated 15 and 16 January 2008, which is over ten years prior to the purported document. The husband’s evidence was that his father had brought the diary from India, from which the paper was extracted. The husband’s father’s evidence did not corroborate his son’s evidence and when asked for an explanation about the discrepancies in the dates, his answer was nonresponsive and incomprehensible.
The document refers to various advances made as referred to above, which total $82,332, interest of 8% payable on the advances and repayment of the funds within 5 years of today, 31 August 2023. The latter date is also curious, because the loan agreement was ostensibly executed on 27 September 2018.
According to the husband, his parents required a formal loan agreement after the advance of $49,975 in May 2018 as his father was concerned it was a significant amount. During cross examination of the husband’s father, there was some vague reference to the magnitude of the amount of $49,975 having been advanced, but generally his answers to difficult questions bore little resemblance to what was actually asked.
The agreement also refers to the funds advanced as having been applied to purchase properties and other family expenses, although it does not specify how much was expended on each category.
The agreement was supposedly witnessed by two friends of the husband, whom the wife acknowledged had visited the family home. There is no reference to the wife being a party to the loan agreement and according to her, nor was she present when it was signed. Indeed, she denied any knowledge of the loan agreement until it was disclosed in the proceeding.
The husband’s evidence was the agreement was signed in City Y, but the witnesses lived in Sydney and were visiting. There was no explanation why the witnesses to the purported loan agreement did not swear affidavits in support of the husband’s version of events and as to their presence at the execution on 27 September 2018.
The husband was cross examined about an application for finance he made to G Bank in July 2020 (Exhibit R-1) and why he did not include the loan owing to his father as a liability in the loan application. He said the finance broker told him he should only include loans to financial institutions.
During cross examination it was put to both the husband and his father they had concocted the loan agreement to increase the liabilities of the husband and wife and accordingly reduce the pool of assets available for distribution between the parties. The husband denied that was the case and the response of the husband’s father was unintelligible.
The wife disputed that any sums so advanced by the husband’s parents were loans and repayable. Her evidence was she only became aware of the loan document relied upon by the husband during the course of the proceedings. As to the cash deposit into her account in May 2015, the wife said the funds were the proceeds of sale of her car, which she sold in India, prior to her moving to Australia in May 2015 and the cash deposit in June 2018 was funds from her family which was brought into the country by a friend.
In her affidavit the wife claimed any funds advanced were gifts and not repayable loans. During her oral evidence in cross examination, she said the funds deposited into the husband’s account by his father were also applied to fund expenses of the husband’s parents whilst they lived with the parties in Australia, as well as travel and visa expenses.
Having considered the evidence of the husband, his father and the wife and bearing in mind my comments as to the credibility of all three witnesses, I do not accept the veracity of the purported loan agreement. I find that the document was of recent construct for the purposes of this proceeding and the amounts advanced by the husband’s father were never intended to be repaid as loans with interest.
I also do not accept the husband’s evidence the $19,000, at line 15 purportedly advanced by his father in 2020 was a loan, because I prefer the evidence of the wife, no documentation was produced by the husband, there were no conversations about the payment of interest and because of my findings about the fabrication of the loan agreement.
Both amounts will be excluded from the balance sheet as liabilities owing to the husband’s father.
I will further refer to the sums advanced in my consideration of contributions.

Husband’s asserted loans from Mr S and Mr U – Items 16 and 17

In the first balance sheet the husband claimed he was indebted to two acquaintances who had supposedly advanced him funds to meet mortgage and other commitments after separation. The husband abandoned these claims when the second balance sheet was provided to the court and the amount recorded as the husband’s liability for each asserted loan was nil. I therefore accept the nil balance of the asserted loans. I observe that in his affidavit of 22 September 2025, the husband deposed that the balance of the loans from his two acquaintances was nil.

Husband’s credit card – Item 18

The husband claimed a credit card liability of $4,288 at separation, which was reflected in the second joint balance sheet. The wife did not address the claimed credit card balance in her affidavit. Because it was included in the second joint balance sheet, and seemingly not disputed by the wife, I will include it as a liability of the husband.
I therefore find the existing assets and liabilities of the husband and wife are as follows:

IS IT JUST AND EQUITABLE TO MAKE PROPERTY SETTLEMENT ORDERS

Section 79(2) of the Act provides:
(2) The court shall not make an order under this section unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order.
In the majority of matters the decision as to whether or not it is just and equitable for the Court to make property orders is resolved by the breakdown of the marital relationship and the mutual applications of the parties to the court for orders altering their respective.
There was no dispute that in the circumstances of this case, it is just and equitable to make property settlement orders and both parties sought property orders. I therefore conclude it is just and equitable to make orders in this case.
I now turn to the relevant statutory considerations.

Sections 79(3)(b)(i) and 79(4) – Contributions

At the commencement of cohabitation neither party had any significant assets. For the first 5 or 6 years after marriage until 2015 the family lived with the husband’s parents in India and received assistance from them, which the husband considers a contribution on his behalf. He also claims his parents funded their wedding celebrations in India.
During their time in India, the husband and wife were engaged in various employment as referred to in each of their trial affidavits. The wife also gave birth to their son in 2010 and despite the husband’s view that his parents rendered significant domestic assistance, I accept the wife was highly involved with the care of their young son. The joint chronology records the wife was employed from July 2011 until May 2015 and undoubtedly contributed financially to the household. The wife’s unchallenged evidence was that she gave her salary to the husband whilst in India
Similarly, the chronology states after the family move to Australia in May 2015, when the child was not quite 5 years old, in April 2016 the wife commenced employment, which generally continued until March 2025. The parties agree the wife gave her salary to the husband from May 2011 until April 2022, although the wife’s evidence was that she regularly gave her salary to the husband. The husband was also employed.
I also accept the wife’s unchallenged evidence about her circumstances upon arrival in Australia, including her undertaking the family’s domestic requirements, care of the child and her attendance at the Adult Migrant English programmes and utilisation of the childcare facilities for their child. I also accept her evidence about her employment from February 2016 onwards.
The husband submits because he earned a higher salary than the wife, he made the majority of financial contributions, and he had access to finance to enable the purchase of the various properties. He does however concede the wife’s income was a direct financial contribution to all properties. That submission misunderstands the holistic approach to assessment and determination of contributions and adjustments (Jabour & Jabour [2019] FamCAFC 78; (2019) FLC 93-898 (“Jabour”) and Horrigan & Horrigan [2020] FamCAFC 25). The holistic approach and rejection of a mathematical approach to assessment of contributions was confirmed by the Full Court in Shinohara at [126] and [133].
Advances from husband’s father during the marriage are referred to in my consideration of whether the advances were a loan from the husband’s parents. I accept the amounts directly transferred into the husband’s bank accounts by his father were so advanced, but I do not accept the husband’s evidence that the cash deposit into the wife’s bank account in June 2018 was funds advanced by the husband’s father. I find the wife’s evidence during cross examination, including about the sale of her car prior to departure for Australia, the retention of some of her Indian salary and some money from her family, to be credible and truthful and in direct contrast to my findings about the constructed loan agreement and the evidence of the husband and his father about the alleged agreement.
As properly conceded by his counsel, the husband did not produce any documentary evidence such as bank statements to support his contentions about the application of the proceeds deposited into his bank account, nor any explanation why he was unable to obtain more recent bank statements or settlement statements to substantiate his claims, when he was able to produce bank statements dating from 2015 and 2018. Absent independent documentary evidence, I view his evidence about the application of the funds, with some caution.
I am able to conclude the funds advanced by the husband’s father into his son’s bank account were disbursed as the husband saw fit. I consider this may well have included some contribution towards acquisition of property or assistance with expenses, but likely also included living expenses for the paternal grandparents, as contended by the wife. Because of the opaque state of the evidence, it is not possible to determine with any precision the extent of the benefit to the husband and wife from the funds so advanced.
The husband contends he made significantly greater contributions than the wife post separation. These include payment of mortgages, for which he ostensibly borrowed funds from his friend Mr S, of which $62,675 was supposedly repaid from the husband from his partial property settlement and a loan from his friend Mr U, who was also repaid $12,000 from the husband’s partial property settlement. The wife disputes the veracity of the loans. Although Mr S swore an affidavit in 2023, neither witness was called by the husband or made available for cross examination and I am able to draw the inference that the evidence of those witnesses was unlikely to assist the husband’s case (Jones v Dunkel (1959) HCA 8).
Irrespective of the veracity of the alleged loans, which I do not accept because of my concerns and caution about accepting the husband’s evidence, the mortgage balances on the B Street property occupied by the wife increased between July 2023 and November 2024 by $40,000[10] and the wife failed to comply with the orders of 24 July 2024 to pay the mortgages on the B Street property. The mortgage repayments of all properties were met from the offset account until the funds were depleted in December 2022. The husband also claims superior contributions because he was primarily responsible for the financial support of the child from around February 2023 when the child refused to return to the wife’s care.
As far as non-financial contributions are concerned, the husband claims he made substantial improvements to the D Street and B Street properties and since July 2024 has also rectified damage cause by the tenants of the F Street property. The husband also claims his father directly assisted with improvements to the properties. The wife denies the husband solely carried out renovations to the properties and contends she also assisted on hands on basis with the property renovations.[11]
Both claim they were the primary carer for the child. The wife asserts she was the child’s primary carer from his birth until recently and the husband claims he was since 2016 when he began to work at home, which enabled the wife to work long hours at her employment. The wife’s unchallenged evidence was that she became pregnant with the child almost immediately and she did not return to work until her son was around nine months old. Thereafter the husband’s parents exerted pressure on her to return to work. Despite that she breastfed the child until he was 3 years old.
The husband claims they were assisted by his parents to care for the child, and the wife claims she contributed to the financial support of the husband’s parents whilst they were in Australia and living in the household.
The wife also relies on s 79(4)(ca) of the Act and contends the family violence she was subjected to, impacted on her capacity to make contributions.
Family violence is defined in s 4AB (1) of the Act. In this case the wife primarily relies upon verbal abuse, threats and coercive and controlling behaviour. The definition was also recently considered by the Full Court in Pickford & Pickford [2024] FedCFamC1A 249; (2024) FLC 94-230 (“Pickford”). The wife deposes a high point of the family violence occurred shortly after having moved to the B Street property. The husband would engage in verbal abuse, which included accusations of the wife having affairs and threats to tell the wife’s father she was a slut. The wife further deposes to being diagnosed with stress and she was referred to a psychologist under a mental health plan shortly thereafter. I am inclined to accept the wife’s version of family violence because her account was not challenged in cross-examination and even though the husband denied these allegations, including never having spoken to the wife’s biological father, I do not hold the husband to be a truthful witness for reasons I have elaborated on earlier.
In her trial affidavit, the wife makes detailed allegations about the husband’s long standing excessive alcohol use, verbal abuse and his coercive and controlling behaviour.
The wife also refers to seeking treatment from a doctor about symptoms she was experiencing as a result of the stress she suffered from the husband’s abusive behaviour.[12] She was referred to a psychologist and prescribed anti-depressants. The joint chronology refers to various intervention orders obtained by the parties. I accept the wife’s evidence in this regard, again because of my findings about the credibility of each of the husband and the wife, the details provided by the wife and the failure to cross examine the wife about the allegations. I have also had regard to the husband’s drink driving convictions, which are referred to in the chronology. I find that the wife’s contributions were rendered more difficult as a result of the husband’s family violence and the consequential effect on the wife.
The husband contended for a contribution adjustment in his favour of 55% and the wife contended for 60% in her favour. After balancing and considering all of the relevant factors, I consider the contributions when taken holistically, should be assessed as equal.

Section 79(5) – current and future circumstances

The husband is aged 41 and enjoys good health. He is currently employed as a professional and earns $98,400 per annum plus overtime. The parties’ child lives with his father, does not spend any time with his mother and has failed to do so since February 2023. The wife alleges the husband has alienated the child from her. In any event, the parties entered into final parenting orders by consent on 17 February 2025.
The husband is responsible for the financial support of their son and currently receives child support from the wife of $44 per month. When she was employed, the wife’s child support liability for the period 1 July 2025 to 31 March 2026 was $900.33 per month, however that assessment ceased in April 2025. According to the husband his expenses for the child are increased because of the child’s professional cricketing aspirations, which require him to pay for interstate and international travel and coaching by professionals. Because the child refuses to spend any time with his mother and the final parenting orders provide for time between them in accordance with the child’s wishes, it is likely the husband will care for the child in the future without any input from the wife.
The wife is aged 40 and is currently unemployed. She ceased employment in February 2025 around the time of the making of the final parenting orders. Prior to then, she was employed by N Company. Her annual salary was $65,000 plus superannuation. A copy of her payslip dated February 2023 is Exhibit A-9. Her most recent salary is significantly lower than the husband’s.
During cross examination the wife said that after the parenting orders she was unable to continue with her previous employment or obtain employment as an educator, because she is highly distressed about the estrangement from her son. Exhibit A-13 is a copy of the wife’s diploma obtained in January 2023. Counsel for the husband asked the wife about her earning capacity and put to her that she has failed to adduce evidence from an appropriately qualified witness that her mental health precluded her obtaining employment. The wife said she had been diagnosed with depression in February 2025 by her General Practitioner, and she had an electronic referral on her phone. The wife also relied on a letter from an accredited mental health social worker dated 10 September 2025, which is located at p 920 of the electronic court book. Counsel for the husband put to the wife the letter, which was displayed on the courtroom screen, did not say that she was unable to work. The letter was referred to in court but was not tendered as an exhibit.
The letter clearly reports the wife struggling with her mental health and obtaining professional assistance and support. The wife’s evidence about her mental health is entirely consistent with the husband’s evidence in his trial affidavit that he believes the wife suffers from untreated mental health issues, has attended a medical centre at City Y, has been on medication for mental health issues even prior to the marriage and was aware she was prescribed medication for depression. In the rubric of that evidence, it is disingenuous for the husband to suggest the wife’s mental health does not affect her capacity for employment. I am able to infer it may well have a very real impact on her on her future capacity to obtain and retain employment in the future, although I accept there was no direct evidence about that issue. If and when she secures employment, I can infer her salary will be significantly less than the husband’s, as was her most recent salary and she will undoubtedly be assessed to pay child support to the husband in accordance with an administrative assessment.
I now turn to consider under s 79(5)(v) of the Act the partial distributions of property made to both parties. Both the husband and the wife received equal distributions of $125,896 from the sale proceeds of the O Street property. Order 1 of the orders of Wilson J made 8 October 2024 provided for the distribution of the proceeds of sale, with such sum to be characterised by the trial judge. During the trial no submissions were made pertaining to the head of power under which that order was made. There was no suggestion that it should be characterised as either lump sum spousal maintenance or pursuant to an exercise of the costs power under the then operative section of the Act, s 117. I therefore conclude the only possible power to make such an order is s 79 of the Act and the distribution will be characterised as a distribution of partial property.
The husband asserts he spent a substantial portion of the amount received by him to repay his father alleged loans and repaying alleged loans he obtained from acquaintances to fund the holding costs of the properties after separation. As referred to above, I do not accept the husband’s assertion of loan liabilities to his father and neither do I accept the loans supposedly advanced by his two acquaintances, neither of whom were presented for cross examination during the trial, and which liabilities the husband agreed to delete from the agreed balance sheet. I accept the submissions of counsel for the wife that Mr S, one of the two friends who had allegedly advanced around $54,500 to the husband, refused to attend court for cross examination, and further, the alleged liabilities were incurred by the husband without the knowledge of the wife. The husband has failed to satisfactorily explain the disposition of his partial property distribution and I intend to take into account in monetary terms, that the husband has had the benefit of $62,896 more than the wife.
The wife retains $63,000 in her bank accounts which sum has been included in the property pool. As referred to above, I intend to take into account under s 79(5)(v) the following:
Description
	
Amount
	
Ownership

Partial property settlement which no longer exists
	
$125,896
	
Husband

Partial property settlement which no longer exists
	
$62,896
	
Wife
The disparity between the parties should be recognised at this juncture and I intend to take into account that the husband has had the benefit of $62,896 more than the wife. As stated by Schonell J in Mihova & Mihova (No 3) [2025] FedCFamC1F 750 at [56] there is nothing preventing a court under s 79(5) of the Act making an adjustment in a monetary sum as opposed to percentage (Waters & Jurek (1995) FLC 92 – 635).
The wife also relied upon s 79(5) (a) and (d) of the Act pertaining to the husband’s use of alcohol and his alleged lavish spending on alcohol. Whilst the husband denied the allegations, the wife was not cross examined or challenged about these allegations. To the contrary, during cross examination about other matters, she frequently referred to the husband’s excessive purchases of alcohol. There was no attempt to quantify the amount, and indeed it would have been almost impossible to do, given the allegations of excessive alcohol consumption dated from shortly after the marriage in 2009. The joint chronology is replete with references to the husband’s drink driving charges and conviction and a compulsory interlock device on the husband’s vehicle. I am able to conclude that during the relationship the husband did drink alcohol beyond the legal limit. Because of my assessment of the wife as a truthful witness and the failure to cross examine her about his issue, I accept her evidence in this regard.
The husband contended for an adjustment in his favour of 10% as did the wife. Taking into account all relevant considerations referred to above and balancing all relevant factors, there should be no adjustment as contended by the husband or the wife and the future needs should be assessed as equal.

CONCLUSION

I therefore conclude the non-superannuation assets of the parties, as determined by me should be divided equally, but with the wife receiving an additional $62,896, which is the monetary value of an adjustment in favour of the wife under s 79(5) taking into account the disparity in funds retained by each party from the partial property settlement. That would result in a payment to the wife of $31,448. To achieve equality and the adjustment in favour of the wife, I will order the husband to pay the wife $31,448.
As to superannuation, I was informed during submissions when the second amended Balance Sheet was handed up, that both parties sought an equalisation of their combined superannuation entitlements and that procedural fairness had been accorded to the trustee of the husband’s superannuation fund. I will therefore make the superannuation orders sought by both parties.
The overall adjustment of non-superannuation assets and superannuation is therefore roughly equal, before the s 79(5) monetary adjustment in favour of the wife. When the monetary adjustment is taken into account, this will result in an overall division of both non superannuation and superannuation assets so that the husband will receive 46.69 percent and the wife will receive 53.31 percent.
The other issue of contention between the parties was whether the wife or the husband should retain the former family home, where the wife has continued to live since separation. The husband asserts he should have the benefit of retaining the property because it has cricket facilities for the parties’ son, and because the son lives with him, he should be able to enjoy that benefit.
Because the wife has lived continuously in the property since 2019 and wishes to continue to live in her home, I consider it appropriate for her to be afforded an opportunity to refinance the mortgage encumbering the property into her name, to enable her to retain the property. I consider 90 days from the date of these orders is sufficient time for her to do so. If the wife is unable to obtain the requisite finance within the 90-day period, the husband shall be afforded an opportunity to secure finance to enable him to retain the property and pay the wife her share. In her proposed order, if she is unable to refinance the mortgage encumbering the property, the wife is content to receive a payment equivalent to the current equity in the property based on the valuation and the mortgage payout as at trial, and I therefore do not propose to make orders providing for the property to be further valued. In his minute of proposed orders, the husband did not make any counter proposal for the quantum of his payment to the wife, if he eventually receives the property.
There were no submissions from either party that the division of property should be adjusted according to the sale proceeds realised after the sale of the D Street and F Street properties, which both parties proposed in their respective minute of orders. I have therefore not taken into account any variation in sale price, either increase or decrease and have adopted the net value as the valuation less the mortgage liability adopted in the joint balance sheet. Further, there was no evidence of sale costs or adjustment of outgoings which might arise from the sale and it is therefore not possible to accurately take such amounts into account. To do so would be speculative.
I have adopted the orders for the mechanics of sale of the D Street and F Street properties as proposed by both parties. Both agree as to the agent, conveyancer, payment of rent into the loan account pending settlement and for the husband to have the right to occupy the D Street property until settlement. Where they differ is the distribution of the sale proceeds. Because the wife will retain the B Street property, if she is able to refinance the property, or alternatively the husband will receive the property and pay to the wife the equity in the B Street property, if she is unable to do so, I have provided , as sought by the wife in her minute of proposed order, for the husband to receive the balance of proceeds of sale of the D Street and F Street properties, except for the payment of $31,448 to the wife.
Because the husband will occupy the F Street property until sale, he will pay the mortgage and outgoings referable to that property until settlement and because he will receive the proceeds of sale of the D Street property, he will be responsible for payment of any shortfall between rent and the mortgage payments and outgoings referable to the D Street property. Because the wife will continue to occupy the B Street property she will pay the mortgage and outgoings referable to that property until settlement.
My determination will result in the husband and wife retaining the following assets and liabilities.
The assets and liabilities the husband will retain are as follows:
